Name: spectral_latent_steering
Title: Spectral Latent Steering: Training-Free Test-Time Optimization via Norm-Preserving Subspace Projection
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that restricting test-time optimization to a low-rank, norm-preserving subspace can achieve efficient and latency-aware inference, while preserving the optimization landscape's geometry.
Related Work: Our proposal builds upon recent works such as Spectral Quantization Routing, Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation, and Geometric Signal Separation, which have demonstrated the effectiveness of spectral methods in training-free inference. However, our approach differs in its focus on norm-preserving subspace projection and its application to test-time optimization.
Abstract: We propose Spectral Latent Steering (SLS), a training-free framework that restricts test-time optimization to a low-rank, norm-preserving subspace. By performing a single-pass PCA on initial layer activations, we identify the dominant spectral directions that govern implicit latent refinement. Subsequent test-time updates are projected onto this subspace, ensuring strict computational budgets while preserving the optimization landscape's geometry. We theoretically ground SLS in information-theoretic bounds, showing that low-rank projections capture the majority of gradient-aligned signal required for reasoning improvement.
Experiments: Evaluate SLS on mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks, comparing its performance to full-space perturbation baselines.
Assess the computational overhead and latency of SLS, measuring its impact on hidden-state perturbation and wall-clock latency.
Investigate the effect of subspace dimensionality on SLS performance, exploring the trade-off between computational efficiency and optimization quality.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The effectiveness of SLS may depend on the quality of the initial layer activations and the choice of subspace dimensionality.
The method may not be suitable for tasks that require significant changes to the optimization landscape, such as those with complex or dynamic objectives.
